tone.description: Authoritative yet accessible financial commentary. Conversational register with analytical depth — uses first person freely, employs colloquial expressions alongside technical jargon. Explanatory and educational in approach.
tone._passiveVoiceNote: 12% is the English source baseline. Target languages may need higher values (e.g. Spanish ~18-20% due to reflexive passive 'se'). Override in per-language glossary scoring if needed.

brandRules: Support/Resistance levels labeled (S1), (S2), (S3) and (R1), (R2), (R3) — maintain these labels in all languages
Support and Resistance levels in ascending numerical order within their category
Currency pairs in standard ISO format without spaces (EUR/USD, GBP/JPY)
Section headers ('The Big Picture', 'Today's highlights') should be translated using glossary terms if available

Abbreviations that STAY in English: Fed, FOMC, BoJ, RBA, RBNZ, BoE, PBoC, NFP, ADP, QQE, JOLTS, RSI, MACD, ECI, ISM, COT, G10. For abbreviations with standard target-language equivalents (CPI, GDP, PPI, bps, qoq, yoy, mom, QE, SAAR) — use the glossary translation.
Hedging language must be preserved in translation — translate 'in my view', 'I believe', 'is likely to', 'could', 'may' with equivalent hedging expressions in the target language
Date format: translate to target language conventions (e.g. '01 de mayo de 2015' in Spanish)
Forex pairs to 4 decimal places, gold to whole numbers, WTI to 2 decimals — preserve numerical precision
Commodity names: translate using glossary if mapping exists, otherwise keep in English (WTI, Brent stay in English)
Idiomatic expressions ('pull the trigger', 'pave the way') should be translated using glossary equivalents if available, or natural target-language idioms
forbiddenTerms: guarantee (used only in the disclaimer phrase 'past performance is no guarantee of future performance' — never used to promise outcomes)
sure/certain/definitely (absolute certainty language avoided in forecasts; hedging language always used instead)
buy/sell recommendations (never uses direct trading instructions; always frames as analytical observations)
we recommend / our recommendation (never provides explicit recommendations)
compliancePatterns: 'past performance is no guarantee of future performance' used when referencing historical patterns — observed in 100% of applicable contexts
Forward-looking statements hedged with modal verbs: 'could', 'is likely to', 'may', 'would' — never stated as certainties — observed in 100% of documents
Technical analysis opinions attributed to analyst personally: 'in my view', 'I believe', 'I would prefer' — observed in 100% of technical sections
Rate expectations attributed to market pricing: 'Fed funds futures show', 'the market gives a 60% probability' — observed in 100% of applicable instances
Economic forecasts attributed to sources: 'the market consensus', 'economists polled by Bloomberg' — observed in 95%+ of forecast references
